64.why: Saves the caller's frame pointer as required by the System V AMD64 callee-save ABI. Every function call pushes an 8-byte return address onto the stack, leaving rsp 8-byte aligned; this push restores 16-byte alignment before any nested calls. Without saving rbp here, the subsequent 'mov rbp, rsp' would overwrite the caller's frame pointer and break debugger stack-unwind chains.

65.why: Snapping rbp to the current stack pointer creates a stable frame base for this activation record. rsp moves with every push/pop/call/ret, but rbp stays fixed, giving debuggers and profilers a reliable anchor to walk the call chain. Some leaf functions omit the frame pointer and use rsp-relative addressing, but any function that calls others should keep rbp for debuggability.

85.why: r12, r13, r14, r15, and rbx are all callee-saved under the System V ABI, meaning this function is free to use them but must restore them before returning. They are chosen here over caller-saved registers (rdi, rsi, rcx, rdx, r8-r11) because the nested calls to str_len_local would silently overwrite those. Parking the haystack pointer in r12 means it survives every call without explicit save/restore around each one.

89.why: rbx is callee-saved and is chosen as the outer loop counter i because it persists across every inner comparison loop and every call to str_len_local. A caller-saved register such as rcx would be overwritten by str_len_local (which uses rcx as its own counter), forcing a stack spill-and-reload around every call.

109.why: XORing a register with itself is the idiomatic zero-idiom in x86-64. The assembler emits the 32-bit form 'xor ebx, ebx' (2 bytes vs 7 for 'mov rbx, 0'), and the CPU automatically zero-extends the 32-bit result into the full 64-bit rbx. It also sets ZF=1 in EFLAGS, which is occasionally useful immediately after the zero. Here it initialises the outer loop index i=0 before the naive search begins.

113.why: LEA (Load Effective Address) computes rbx+r15 (i+m) as pure integer arithmetic without touching memory or flags. It is used here to check the boundary condition i+m <= n before starting an inner comparison. Using lea instead of 'add rax, r15' after a 'mov rax, rbx' preserves any flags that may be live from a previous cmp, and avoids a temporary register.

118.why: Resets the inner-loop character index j to zero at the start of each new alignment attempt. rcx is caller-saved and may hold any value left over from the previous iteration or from the str_len_local calls; the explicit xor guarantees j starts clean for every candidate starting position i.

124.why: i+j is the byte offset into the haystack for the current character comparison. LEA computes the sum as an address expression without dereferencing memory — the actual byte load happens on the next line using rax as a displacement from the haystack base pointer r12. This keeps the addressing clean and avoids a separate add before the memory operand.

148.why: The stack is strictly LIFO, so registers must be popped in the exact reverse of their push order. rbx was the last register pushed in the prologue (lines 85-89), so it must be the first popped in the epilogue. Reversing the order incorrectly — even by one slot — silently installs wrong values into registers and can corrupt the saved return address, causing a crash on ret.

153.why: Restores the caller's frame pointer saved at the function entry. After this pop, rsp points exactly at the 8-byte return address that 'call strstr_naive' deposited. The ret on the next line pops that address and transfers control back to the call site. Any push/pop imbalance between prologue and epilogue would make ret jump to garbage.

178.why: fail[0] is always 0 by definition: a single character has no proper prefix that is also a suffix. This explicit store is the base case that the iterative table-building loop (starting at i=1) relies on — when the fallback chain eventually reaches k=0, it looks up fail[0]=0 to determine there is no further prefix to fall back to. Without this initialisation the entry would hold BSS garbage and the fallback could run off the end of the table.

197.why: k-1 is the index needed to look up the previous failure value when a mismatch occurs and k>0. LEA computes the subtraction into a scratch register without modifying rcx, which must retain the current k for the subsequent table load and potential retry. Using 'dec rcx' would permanently alter k before the lookup, breaking the retry logic.

198.why: This implements the KMP failure-function fallback: when pattern[i] mismatches pattern[k] and k>0, k jumps to fail[k-1] and the comparison retries at position i without advancing i. The *8 scale converts the int64_t array index (rax = k-1) to a byte offset. This is the heart of KMP's O(n+m) guarantee — it reuses the longest border already matched instead of restarting from position 0.

249.why: Writing a static label without brackets places its address (like '&fail_table' in C) into rdx rather than loading the value stored there. rdx is the third argument register in the System V calling convention, so this passes the pointer to the BSS output buffer as the third parameter of build_kmp_table. The function will write 8-byte entries into that array during table construction.

265.why: When haystack[i] mismatches pattern[j], KMP normally falls back to fail[j-1]. But if j is already 0 there is no prefix to fall back to — this haystack character simply cannot extend any partial match. This test guards against an invalid fail[-1] lookup and forces i to advance instead, which is the only correct action at the start of the pattern.

269.why: j-1 is the index into the failure table for the fallback step. LEA computes it into a scratch register without touching rcx (j), which the main search loop will re-read as the updated j after the jmp. If rcx were decremented directly here, the fallback value would be loaded with the wrong index on every mismatch.

270.why: Loads fail[j-1] — the length of the longest proper border of pattern[0..j-1]. Setting j to this value means the next iteration compares the current haystack character (i does not move) against pattern[fail[j-1]]. This is the maximum position in the pattern from which a match could still succeed, exploiting the prefix work already done. The *8 scale factor converts the element index to a byte offset for the int64_t array.

281.why: rcx holds j (pattern characters matched so far) and r15 holds m (pattern length). When j == m every character of the pattern has matched consecutively, so this single comparison is the complete-match detector. The match start is then recovered as i-m without having needed a separate 'match_start' variable throughout the search loop.

286.why: After a full match, rbx (copied into rax) holds the haystack index one past the last matched character. Subtracting the pattern length m recovers the 0-based start index of the match. Tracking only the end position and computing the start on exit is simpler than maintaining a separate 'start' variable inside the search loop.
